content:
# Luke Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 88-89

---

**1** And Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness

**2** for forty days, being tempted by the devil. He ate nothing in those days, and when they were completed, he was hungry.

**3** The devil said to him, "If you are the Son of God, command this stone to become bread."

**4** And Jesus answered him, "It is written, 'Man shall not live by bread alone.'"

**5** And leading him up, the devil showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time. **6** And the devil said to him, "To you I will give all this authority and their glory, for it has been delivered to me, and to whomever I wish I give it. **7** If you, therefore, will worship before me, it shall all be yours."

**8** And Jesus answered him, "It is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and him alone shall you serve.'"

**9** Then he led him to Jerusalem and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple and said to him, "If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here, **10** for it is written, 'He shall command his angels concerning you, to guard you,' **11** and, 'On their hands they shall bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone.'"

**12** And Jesus answered him, "It has been said, 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.'"

**13** And when the devil had exhausted every temptation, he departed from him until an opportune time.

**14** And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit to Galilee, and a report went out through the whole surrounding region concerning him. **15** And he taught in their synagogues, being glorified by all.

**16** And he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, and he entered the synagogue on the Sabbath day, as was his custom, and stood up to read. **17** The scroll of the prophet Isaiah was given to him, and having unrolled the scroll, he found the place where it was written:

**18** *"The Spirit of the Lord is upon me,*
*because he has anointed me*
*to proclaim good news to the poor.*
*He has sent me to announce release to the captives*
*and recovery of sight to the blind,*
*to set free those who are oppressed,*
**19** *to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord."*

**20** And having rolled up the scroll, he gave it back to the attendant and sat down. And the eyes of all in the synagogue were fixed upon him. **21** And he began to say to them, "Today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing."

**22** And all spoke well of him and marveled at the gracious words that came from his mouth, and they said, "Is not this the son of Joseph?"

**23** And he said to them, "Surely you will quote this proverb to me: 'Physician, heal yourself! Whatever we heard was done in Capernaum, do here also in your own homeland.'"

**24** And he said, "Truly I say to you, no prophet is accepted in his own homeland.

**25** "But in truth I tell you, there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah, when the heaven was shut up for three years and six months, while a great famine came upon all the land, **26** and to none of them was Elijah sent, except to a widow in Zarephath of Sidon. **27** And there were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet, and none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian."

**28** And all in the synagogue were filled with fury when they heard these things. **29** They rose up and drove him out of the city, and led him to the brow of the hill on which their city had been built, so as to hurl him down the cliff. **30** But he, passing through the midst of them, went on his way.

**31** And he went down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and was teaching them on the Sabbath. **32** And they were astounded at his teaching, for his word carried authority.

**33** And in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon, and he cried out with a loud voice, **34** "Ha! What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God!"

**35** And Jesus rebuked him, saying, "Be silent and come out of him!" And the demon, having thrown him down in the midst, came out of him without doing him any harm.

**36** And amazement came upon all, and they said to one another, "What is this word? For with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out!" **37** And a report about him went out into every place of the surrounding region.

**38** And he rose up from the synagogue and entered the house of Simon. Now Simon's mother-in-law was gripped by a severe fever, and they appealed to him on her behalf. **39** And standing over her, he rebuked the fever, and it left her. Immediately she rose up and began to serve them.

**40** As the sun was setting, all who had anyone sick with various diseases brought them to him, and he laid his hands on each one of them and healed them. **41** And demons also came out of many, crying out and saying, "You are the Son of God!" But he rebuked them and would not allow them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ.

**42** And when day came, he went out to a desolate place. The crowds searched for him, and when they came to him they tried to keep him from leaving them. **43** But he said to them, "I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other cities as well, for that is why I was sent."

**44** And he was preaching in the synagogues of Judea.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification
Both manuscript pages (LUK_004_page_001.jpg, LUK_004_page_002.jpg) were examined. Page 1 (folio 243v) contains the end of Luke 3's genealogy and the beginning of chapter 4 in columns 3-4. Page 2 (folio 244r) continues through the remainder of chapter 4 across four columns, concluding at verse 44. The uncial majuscule script is consistent with Sinaiticus. Column annotations at the bottom of the pages confirm the verse ranges (Lk. 3:21, 3:30, 4:1, 4:9 on page 1; Lk. 4:18, 4:25, 4:34, 4:40 on page 2).

### Consensus Methodology
All three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) were working from the same Codex Sinaiticus text and agreed on every major textual decision. Differences between them were matters of English style, not manuscript reading. SOPHIA's consensus aims for clarity and dignity of expression, drawing on LOGOS's formal precision as the primary register while incorporating PNEUMA's readability and EMET's structural fidelity where they improve the result.

### Key Textual Decisions (unanimous across all three agents)

1. **Verse 4 — Shorter reading adopted.** All three agents follow Sinaiticus in omitting "but by every word of God." This shorter text is supported by Vaticanus and is the probable original, with the longer Byzantine reading being a harmonization with Matthew 4:4 / Deuteronomy 8:3.

2. **Verse 5 — Lukan temptation order preserved.** The kingdoms temptation stands second (vv. 5-8) and the temple-pinnacle third (vv. 9-12), following Sinaiticus and the Alexandrian tradition, differing from Matthew's sequence.

3. **Verse 8 — "Get behind me, Satan" omitted.** All three agents omit this phrase, absent from Sinaiticus and Vaticanus, and widely regarded as a scribal importation from Matthew 4:10.

4. **Verse 17 — "having unrolled" (anaptuxas).** Sinaiticus reads the verb for unrolling a scroll. All three agents preserve this reading, reflecting the historical scroll format.

5. **Verse 18 — Composite Isaiah quotation.** The quotation blends Isaiah 61:1-2 with Isaiah 58:6 ("to set free those who are oppressed"). All agents preserve Luke's composite citation as found in Sinaiticus.

6. **Verse 27 — "Naaman."** Sinaiticus spells the name NAIMAN. SOPHIA normalizes to the conventional English "Naaman" (with LOGOS and PNEUMA), while EMET preserves the Sinaiticus orthography "Naiman." See Disagreement Log.

7. **Verse 34 — "Ha!" (EA).** All three agents render the exclamation EA as "Ha!" — preserving the demonic cry of alarm found in Sinaiticus.

8. **Verse 44 — "Judea" retained.** All three agents follow Sinaiticus's reading "synagogues of Judea" rather than the Byzantine variant "Galilee." "Judea" here likely functions as a broad geographical term for Jewish territory, not specifically the southern province. This is the lectio difficilior and is supported by Vaticanus.

### Stylistic Choices

- **Register:** SOPHIA adopts a moderately formal English register — dignified but not archaic, accessible but not colloquial.
- **"Homeland" over "fatherland":** In verses 23-24, SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA in rendering PATRIS as "homeland" rather than EMET's "fatherland," which carries unintended modern connotations.
- **Isaiah quotation formatting:** SOPHIA follows PNEUMA's poetic line formatting for the Isaiah quotation (vv. 18-19), since it represents Scripture read aloud in a liturgical setting.
- **Verse 13 — "exhausted every temptation":** SOPHIA blends PNEUMA's dynamic "exhausted" with LOGOS's structure, producing "when the devil had exhausted every temptation" — clearer than EMET's literal "having completed every temptation" and more precise than a pure paraphrase.
- **Verse 38 — "gripped by a severe fever":** A consensus between EMET's "being-held by a great fever" (too wooden), LOGOS's "afflicted with a great fever" (adequate), and PNEUMA's "suffering from a severe fever" (good). "Gripped" captures the Greek's sense of being seized/held while remaining vivid.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Ruling | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | "full of the Holy Spirit" | "full of [the] Holy Spirit" | "full of the Holy Spirit" | "overflowing with the Holy Spirit" | "full of the Holy Spirit" | PNEUMA's "overflowing" adds interpretive intensity not in the Greek *pleres*. The standard rendering "full of" is accurate and shared by EMET/LOGOS. |
| 2 | "hungered" vs "was hungry" vs "was starving" | "he hungered" | "he hungered" | "he was starving" | "he was hungry" | PNEUMA's "starving" is hyperbolic beyond the Greek *epeinasen*. EMET/LOGOS's "hungered" is slightly archaic. "Was hungry" is natural and faithful. |
| 3 | "speak to this stone" vs "command" vs "tell" | "speak to this stone" | "command this stone" | "tell this stone" | "command this stone" | Greek *eipe* (say/speak) functions here as a command. LOGOS's "command" captures the force; EMET is too literal; PNEUMA too informal. |
| 5 | "inhabited-world" vs "world" | "kingdoms of the inhabited-world" | "kingdoms of the world" | "kingdoms of the world" | "kingdoms of the world" | EMET's "inhabited-world" (for *oikoumene*) is technically precise but awkward in English. "World" conveys the scope adequately. |
| 13 | "until a season" vs "opportune time" | "until a season" | "until an opportune time" | "until a more opportune time" | "until an opportune time" | Greek *achri kairou* means "until a [fitting] season." EMET is too bare; PNEUMA's "more" adds a comparative not in the text. LOGOS strikes the right balance. |
| 21 | "in your ears" vs "in your hearing" | "fulfilled in your ears" | "fulfilled in your hearing" | "right here as you listen, this scripture has come true" | "fulfilled in your hearing" | EMET's "in your ears" is overly literal; PNEUMA paraphrases too freely. LOGOS's "in your hearing" faithfully renders the Greek idiom in natural English. |
| 22 | "bearing-witness" vs "bore witness" vs "spoke well of" | "all were bearing-witness to him" | "all bore witness to him" | "everyone spoke well of him" | "all spoke well of him" | Greek *emarturoun* here carries the sense of commending/affirming (positive testimony), not legal witnessing. PNEUMA captures the contextual meaning best. |
| 23 | "parable" vs "proverb" | "this parable" | "this parable" | "this proverb" | "this proverb" | Greek *parabole* can mean proverb, comparison, or parable. In context, "Physician, heal yourself" is a proverbial saying, not a narrative parable. PNEUMA's choice is more contextually accurate. |
| 27 | Spelling of the Syrian commander | "Naiman" | "Naaman" | "Naaman" | "Naaman" | Sinaiticus spells NAIMAN, but the conventional English form from 2 Kings 5 is "Naaman." EMET's transliteration is manuscript-faithful but would confuse readers. Normalizing to the established English form is appropriate. |
| 29 | "city" vs "town" | "city" | "city" | "town" | "city" | Greek *polis* is consistently "city." While Nazareth was small, the Greek term is *polis* and should be rendered consistently. EMET and LOGOS are correct. |
| 32 | "his word was with authority" vs "carried authority" | "his word was with authority" | "his word was with authority" | "his words carried authority" | "his word carried authority" | SOPHIA combines PNEUMA's more dynamic verb "carried" with the singular "word" (*logos*) preserved by EMET/LOGOS, yielding a reading that is both faithful and vivid. |
| 38 | "asked" vs "made request" vs "asked Jesus to help" | "they asked him concerning her" | "they made request of him concerning her" | "they asked Jesus to help her" | "they appealed to him on her behalf" | All three are adequate but none ideal. SOPHIA's phrasing captures the urgency of *erotao* (to ask/entreat) while maintaining the indirect construction of the Greek. |
